body_txt: A logical fallacy , specifically a sub-type of Association Fallacy , that assumes that anything done or liked by a bad person must be bad itself, taking things to absurd levels. The premise seems to be that bad people must have a way to tell if something is evil . Either that, or bad people are repulsed by anything that isn't at least as evil as they are . Whatever the reason, bad people magically will only associate with things that are also bad. Therefore, people claim a thing is bad because bad people associate with it. This is a concept called the Association Fallacy (or the more well-known term "Guilt by Association"), which often overlaps with Godwin's Law due to how often Adolf Hitler is used ( also known as Reductio ad Hitlerum ). After all, Hitler has the reputation of being the very embodiment of evil in history. So, he supposedly would only do/like/own things that are as evil as himself. You know, things like sitting on a chair , wearing clothes, eating, taking a walk, and breathing. Hitler did those things, but that doesn't make them bad. Hitler is not the reason things are bad. We don't think committing mass murder is bad because Hitler, Stalin, or other bad people did it. We think those people are bad because they committed mass murder. In other words, this trope is backward. A thing being bad stands on its own as bad. It would be like:  This is also one of the reasons why we are often loath to admit that a person who we hate may actually be right for once . Remember, Don't Shoot the Message . Someone intimidated by this "argument" may invoke No True Scotsman as a "rebuttal" ("Hitler wasn't a REAL vegetarian"). For similar reasons, this trope also has been known to overlap with You Know Who Said That? , when a person says a quote that other people would agree with at face value, but then—gotcha!—reveals Hitler or some other contemptible figure articulated it first. Of course, as explained, that doesn't necessarily prove the idea is immoral on its own merits. On the other hand, if it's framed very carefully there can be a valid point to be made here. If something is demonstrably evil on its own terms, then pointing out how it's tied to an evil source is relevant as a supporting example, not as a proof. It might even serve as a sort of moral wake-up call . That would be like:  A particularly insidious individual can invert this to normalize abhorrent behavior. If someone points out that a person or government is carrying out evil actions comparable to the Nazis, the accused can simply claim that their critics are employing this trope . The insinuation is that the Nazis weren't wrong to practice the actions the present party is accused of perpetrating now and that the accusation is spurious. A Sub-Trope of So Was X , Anti-Advice , and the Association Fallacy . A Sister Trope to Abomination Accusation Attack (attacking people by accusing them of doing or wanting to do something inexcusably atrocious), Ad Hominem (refuting an argument by insulting an aspect of the presenter that isn't relevant to the argument's content), Chewbacca Defense (attempting to win a debate using an irrelevant logical fallacy), Godwin's Law (insinuating that a person is bad by comparing them to Hitler and the Nazis), Damned by a Fool's Praise (insinuating that something is bad because a stupid or unpleasant person likes it), Space Whale Aesop (a moral that involves bad actions having ludicrously unrealistic consequences), and Bulverism (contesting someone's motives for believing an idea without explaining why the idea is wrong in the first place). Often used as one of the less spurious links in a chain of Insane Troll Logic . This trope also has a page on Wikipedia , where it is referred to as Reductio ad Hitlerum. This trope is based on very similar thinking to No True Scotsman , as both are logical fallacies used to exclude or stigmatize something. It could also be seen as something of an Inverted Trope : No True Scotsman fallaciously excludes one bad example to keep people from identifying the broader group with it, while Hitler Ate Sugar fallaciously goes right ahead and identifies the broader group with the one bad example. An alternate form of inversion is What Would X Do? , when people argue that an action is good solely on the grounds that a good person did it. Yet another inversion would be Even Evil Has Standards , where a widely hated villain's dis approval is taken as evidence that something must be really bad. Unrelated to Real Men Hate Sugar , where people come to hate something normally considered pleasant due to cultural expectations of what is considered "mature" or "masculine". If Hitler is shown hating sugar, it would likely be a case of Bad Is Good and Good Is Bad , but still counting as this trope if sugar is portrayed as good entirely based on Hitler's dislike of it as a result. As such, unless it is used to demonize sugar and the people who eat it, this trope also has nothing to do with Hitler's well-documented fondness for sweets . See Villains Out Shopping for examples of evildoers doing innocuous things.     Harry Potter Miraculous Ladybug My Little Pony: Friendship Is Magic The Owl House Superman
